region_of_origin: China (Sichuan / Northwest China; served in Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for cold dressed / marinated beef. FOODON_03302051 (beef food product) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKL (Beef, cooked) is the closest parent; no specific code for cold-dressed marinated beef exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226934003 (Beef, food) used as closest parent; no specific concept for cold dressed beef exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of cold dressed beef is approximately 150 g, presented as thinly sliced chilled beef dressed with a seasoned sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, shank, cooked, simmered — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 13356); adapted with dressing components from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods (soy sauce, sesame oil, chilli oil)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy beef shank (cooked, simmered), which closely represents the lean boiled beef used in this dish. Dressing components (soy sauce, sesame oil, chilli oil, garlic) contribute additional sodium, fat, and minor carbohydrate. Values adjusted proportionally to reflect a typical cold-dressed preparation at approximately 60% lean beef and 40% dressing contribution by weight impact on macros. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate recalculated accordingly. This is an adapted estimate; exact restaurant formulation is unknown.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cold dressed beef. Beef and other pure protein-dominant meat dishes are consistently assigned GI values in the range of 0–15 in peer-reviewed GI literature (Atkinson et al. 2021; Foster-Powell et al. 2002). A GI of 10 is used as a conservative low estimate for this high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat dish. GL per 150 g serving = (10 × 2.8 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.42, rounded to 0.4. gi_category is low.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning dressing
hypertension.guidance: Cold dressed beef contains approximately 520 mg sodium per 100 g, primarily from soy sauce and seasoning; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 80–100 g and request reduced-sodium dressing to keep sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a GI of approximately 10, cold dressed beef has a negligible glycemic load and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions, though the sodium content from the dressing warrants attention in those with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium from seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cold dressed beef provides moderate saturated fat (2.4 g/100 g) and high sodium (520 mg/100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose leaner cuts, and request lighter dressing to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cold dressed beef (凉拌牛肉, liáng bàn niúròu) is a classic Chinese cold appetiser dish in which lean beef — typically shank or brisket — is first simmered or braised until tender, then chilled, thinly sliced, and dressed with a flavourful sauce. The dressing typically combines soy sauce, sesame oil, chilli oil, garlic, ginger, Sichuan peppercorn, vinegar, and a small amount of sugar, producing a complex balance of savoury, spicy, numbing, and slightly tangy flavours. The texture is firm yet tender, with a silky coating from the sesame and chilli oils. This dish is widely served as a starter or side dish in Chinese restaurants across Singapore, including Lanzhou beef noodle establishments where it accompanies noodle soups. Nutritionally, cold dressed beef is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate food with approximately 19.5 g protein and 9.2 g fat per 100 g. Its glycemic index is very low (estimated GI ~10), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The primary clinical concern is sodium content (~520 mg/100 g) from the soy-based dressing, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is a good source of zinc, vitamin B12, iron, and niacin. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans and contains soy and sesame allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in cold dressed beef?
Is cold dressed beef good for diabetics?
Is cold dressed beef high in sodium?
Cold beef side dish Lanzhou noodles
meal_context: Side dish with Lanzhou beef noodles

confidence_reason: No exact match for cold dressed beef found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy beef shank (cooked, simmered) with proportional adjustment for dressing components. GI is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed GI literature for high-protein meat dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base ingredient (cooked beef shank) is well-characterised in USDA data and the dressing contribution is a standard Chinese cold-dish formulation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on USDA SR Legacy NDB 13356 (beef shank, cooked, simmered) with dressing component adjustment; exact restaurant formulation at Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles is unknown. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food item. 3. GI of 10 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for cold dressed beef exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 4. Halal status is listed as false because beef sourcing and preparation at the specific restaurant cannot be confirmed from available data; consumers should verify with the restaurant. 5. The cuisine tag 'Drinks & Beverages' in the input source data appears to be a field survey platform categorisation artefact and has been disregarded. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked beef shank and are not independently verified for this specific restaurant dish. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source for beef shank cooked.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record from USDA SR Legacy beef shank data with dressing adaptation. Added all multilingual names for Singaporean cuisine requirement. Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 sentinel value in notes. Set GI to 10 (numeric > 0) with analogous estimate documentation. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. All hard gates and Q1-Q8 criteria evaluated as passing.
